text.title: Rate Limiting Antipatterns
text.summary: In-memory limiters at scale, CDN IP collision, IPv6 bypass, double-limiting, fixed-window burst, and token-bucket clamp omission.

antipattern.description: In-memory (dict-based) rate limiter deployed with more than one replica.
antipattern.reason: Each replica has independent state. A user can hit N replicas sequentially and send N times the configured limit before any single replica records a violation. Always use Redis/Valkey for distributed state.

antipattern.description: Per-IP rate limiter behind a CDN using request.client.host for the key.
antipattern.reason: Behind a CDN, request.client.host is the CDN edge IP. All users share one counter; when any user hits the limit, all users are throttled. Parse X-Forwarded-For with a trusted-proxy list.
IPv6 users can rotate IPs within their /64 subnet trivially. Per-address limits allow effectively unlimited requests by rotating through the /64 address space.

antipattern.description: Double-limiting: edge (Cloudflare/Kong) AND application-layer limiters on the same route without coordination.
antipattern.reason: Edge and app limits may have different windows and counts, causing confusing 429s that don't match the documented limits. Designate one canonical layer per route and document it.

antipattern.description: Token bucket without min(bucket_size, ...) clamp on refill.
antipattern.reason: Without the clamp, long idle periods accumulate unlimited tokens. A user idle for 1 hour against a 100/s bucket can burst 360,000 requests, defeating the bucket size limit entirely.

antipattern.description: Agent silently raises a limit because a load test failed.
antipattern.reason: Limits encode security and cost decisions, not just performance ones. Raising a limit because a load test hit it may open an abuse vector. Human review is required for any limit change on auth and billing endpoints.

antipattern.description: Missing integration test for the 429 path.
antipattern.reason: The happy path is always tested; the 429 path is usually not. Broken Retry-After parsing in clients is a common source of retry storms. Always test the 429 response and its headers in integration tests.
